coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots by means of a simple room-temperature synthesis using BSA protein and nucleosides or amino acids
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Aparicio-Ixta, L.
Pichardo-Molina, J.L.
Cardoso-Avila, P.E.
Ojeda-Galvan, H.J.
Martínez-García, M.M.
coredata.dc:description: There is considerable interest in the synthesis and applications of carbon dots due to their intriguing tunable optical properties, among which are strong photobleaching resistance and low toxicity for biomedical applications in sensing and biosensing. C-dots are prepared using different fabrication methods that use carbon-rich materials, including carbohydrates, citric acid, proteins, plants and organic waste materials. In this work, we report the synthesis of nitrogen co-doped carbon dots, which is carried out through a simple and quick method at room temperature, with serum bovine albumin as the carbon source, and an amino acid (L-Arginine) or a nucleoside (Adenosine, Cytidine) to be added during the synthesis as the nitrogen source and then crosslinked to surface carbon dots by glutaraldehyde. After twenty hours of synthesis, the solution turned orange, indicating the complete formation of highly stable fluorescent C-dots, characterized by UV−Vis, fluorescence, FTIR, XPS spectroscopies and SEM. The resulting C-dots were all fluorescents, with a mean size of 5 nm, exhibiting the following functional groups on the C-dots surface: CN, NH, CN and CO, confirming the co-doping with nitrogen. Among the prepared samples, the addition of Cytidine yields the strongest emissive colloid, showing a robust yellow fluorescence (maximum emission at 560 nm), with a high quantum yield of 31.4% and good photobleaching resistance to green laser (117 mW/cm2).
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-dots
Fluorescence
Bovine serum albumin
Nucleoside
Amino acid

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the Material Characterization Lab (CIO) and M Christian Albort for her technical help, National Laboratory of Nano and Biomaterials (CINVESTAV, Merida, Yuc. Mex.) for their facilities for XPS analysis, Ph.D. G. Ramos for fluorometer access (CIO), Jesus Aparicio Ixta for help in abstract design and Mario Ruiz Verganza for proofreading the manuscript.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photograph of the different C-dots suspensions under daylight (panel a), and the chemical diagrams of the N-dopant molecules used (panel b).
HRTEM micrographs of C-dots: a) A-Cdots, b) B-Cdots, c) C-Cdots, d) B-Cdots-Cy, and e) C-Cdots-Cy.
a) FTIR spectra of C-Cdots, C-Cdots-Ad, C-Cdots-LA, and C-Cdots-Cy; b) XPS spectra of A-Cdots, C-Cdots, B-Cdots-Cy, and C-Cdots-Cy.
UV-Vis absorption spectra of C-dot samples from: a) batch-A, b) batch-B and c) batch-C; the inset shows the maximum absorbance for all C-dot samples.

a) Photoluminescence spectra, (b-e) fluorescence emission maps for the C-Cdots-j, and f) fluorescence quantum yield of Cdots, Cdots-Ad, Cdots-LA and Cdots-Cy fabricated using the original synthesis (Batch-A), replacing water for ethanol (Batch-B), and finally, samples fabricated without ethanol and no additional water (Batch-C).
Fluorescence decay curves of C-Cdots and C-Cdots-j, where j = Ad, LA or Cy, (excitation wavelength of 510 nm).
Photograph of the different C-dot (i-Cdots-j) colloids under green light (532 nm).
Kinetics of 532 nm laser diode induced fluorescence photobleaching of colloids at a concentration of 0.4 W% C-dots in water.
The table shows a summary of the relative percentage values of C, O, N and Na determined through XPS.
Main optical properties of the three batches of C-dots.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: * λmax-1,λmax-2 are the wavelengths of maximum absorption of bands one and two.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots by means of a simple room-temperature synthesis using BSA protein and nucleosides or amino acids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Applied Physics Department, Cinvestav-Unidad Mérida, Carretera Antigua a Progreso Km. 6, Mérida, Yucatán 97217, Mexico

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Applied Physics Department, Cinvestav-Unidad Mérida, Carretera Antigua a Progreso Km. 6

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Applied Physics Department, CINVESTAV-IPN Mérida, Mérida, México

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There is considerable interest in the synthesis and applications of carbon dots due to their intriguing tunable optical properties, among which are strong photobleaching resistance and low toxicity for biomedical applications in sensing and biosensing. C-dots are prepared using different fabrication methods that use carbon-rich materials, including carbohydrates, citric acid, proteins, plants and organic waste materials. In this work, we report the synthesis of nitrogen co-doped carbon dots, which is carried out through a simple and quick method at room temperature, with serum bovine albumin as the carbon source, and an amino acid (L-Arginine) or a nucleoside (Adenosine, Cytidine) to be added during the synthesis as the nitrogen source and then crosslinked to surface carbon dots by glutaraldehyde. After twenty hours of synthesis, the solution turned orange, indicating the complete formation of highly stable fluorescent C-dots, characterized by UV−Vis, fluorescence, FTIR, XPS spectroscopies and SEM. The resulting C-dots were all fluorescents, with a mean size of 5 nm, exhibiting the following functional groups on the C-dots surface: CN, NH, CN and CO, confirming the co-doping with nitrogen. Among the prepared samples, the addition of Cytidine yields the strongest emissive colloid, showing a robust yellow fluorescence (maximum emission at 560 nm), with a high quantum yield of 31.4% and good photobleaching resistance to green laser (117 mW/cm2).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: There is a great interest in developing new methods and synthesis strategies to fabricate C-dots from starting materials such as biowastes, natural sources, waste materials and diverse organic molecules [1]. The main constituents of C-dots are carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen with lower content [2–7]. The primary strategy to increase nitrogen content is by doping the C-dots with some nitrogen-containing functional groups, which could introduce defective sites by replacing carbon atoms, providing potential active sites to enrich the chemical activities of C-dots [8–11]. Nucleobases, nucleosides and nucleotides, in addition to being raw materials to obtain N-dopping C-dots, have interesting metal-binding properties, and thus have been used for sensing applications [12,13]. For example, Li and coworkers formed C-dots with adenosine monophosphate through hydrothermal treatment [14]. Conversely, Zhou and coworkers prepared a series of C-dots with citrate as the carbon source and ethylenediamine, adenosine, cytidine, thymidine or guanosine as the respective nitrogen sources [15]. Jingfang Shangguan reported ultra-bright N/P co-doped carbon dots by employing adenosine 5′-triphosphate (ATP) as a selective fluorescent nanoprobe for Fe3+ detection in biological samples [9]. Mingqian Wang reported fluorescent biomimetic carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) developed via pyrolysis of cytidine diphosphate choline (CDPC) and ethylenediamine; the BCQDs showed emission at a wavelength of 450 nm, along with a modest quantum yield of 25% [16].

Despite the development of synthetic routes, the luminescence properties of C-dots are generally modest, showing low fluorescent quantum yields, poor size control, high energy cost for its fabrication and the need for exhaustive purification, limiting some applications of C-dots [13,17–21]. Typically, C-dots fabricated from natural derivatives/biomass which were fabricated using a hydrothermal or microwave method exhibit a quantum yield of fluorescence below 18%, while the C-dots prepared from various organic molecules have a quantum yield of 27% on average [1]. Thus, there is a great need to develop a facile, low-cost, environment-friendly, sustainable, high-yield method for preparing C-dots with robust fluorescent emission and high quantum yields for applications like light emitting diodes, photocatalysis, solar cells, or fluorescence-based sensors [22,23].

Herein, we report a novel approach to converting bovine serum albumin (BSA) protein by adding nucleotides or amino acids, potential biocompatible starting precursors, into C-dots. We propose an eco-friendly, economical wet green-chemical procedure to obtain C-dots co-doped with nitrogen to improve the quantum yield, and to provide good resistance of photo-bleaching to laser excitation. The method also avoids the use of ethanol reported in the previous article [24], and now it employs adenosine (Ad), L-Arginine (LA) and cytidine (Cy) as nitrogen sources during the synthesis, improving the quantum yield up to 34.1%.

Bovine serum albumin (BSA, lyophilized powder, 96%), Sodium hydroxide (98%), Glutaraldehyde (Grade II, 25% in H2O), Adenosine, L-Arginine and Cytidine were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, and Ethyl alcohol (99.5%) was purchased from Karal. The water used in the experiments was obtained from a MilliQ© Direct system.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained with an Agilent Carry 5000 UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence excitation-emission maps, absolute quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime were measured on an Edinburg Instruments FS5 ﬂuorometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on a K-AlphaTM + X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer System. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired using a Cary670 FTIR spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). A JEM-2100 JEOL high-resolution transmission electron microscope was used to examine the morphologies of the C-dots with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

C-dots were synthesized by means of a slight modification of a previously reported method [24]. In a typical synthesis, 8 mL of a BSA solution (0.5% M/V) were prepared by magnetic stirring at 500 rpm, at room temperature (22 °C); then, 1 mL of NaOH (0.2 M) was mixed under vigorous magnetic stirring to get a pH of 11, and after 10 min, we started the dropwise injection of 16 mL of ethanol, at a 10 mL/min rate, using a home-made syringe pump. Then, five minutes after adding all the ethanol, 160 µL of glutaraldehyde solution (GA, 25%) was added under magnetic agitation to get it well mixed. Finally, one of the nucleosides adenosine (20 mg) or cytidine (195 mg), or the amino acid L-Arginine (139 mg) was immediately added, and nitrogen-doped C-dots were obtained after twenty hours of continuous agitation.

Three batches of samples were prepared with slight modifications in the following form: samples of batch-A were prepared exactly as the original synthesis, samples of batch-B were prepared by replacing the 16 mL of ethanol with 16 mL of water, and finally, samples of batch-C were prepared without ethanol and no additional water. The samples will be labeled in the following form i-Cdots-j, where i = A, B, C means the sample of the corresponding batch, and j = LA, Cy and Ad, with LA, Cy and Ad refering to L-Arginine, Cytidine and Adenosine respectively; when index j is absent, it means that only BSA was used.

The color change of the suspensions was taken as indicative of the successful preparation of C-dots, which was carried out without using any harmful organic reagents. The purification consisted of centrifugating each colloid at 17,000 rpm for one hour, with the precipitated solids being negligible and not visible by bare sight; on the other hand, the supernatants were colored and not dispersive, so they were used for the fluorescence analysis. Supernatants of samples prepared without ethanol were lyophilized to obtain C-dots powders for the other analyses (XPS, TEM, and FTIR).

As can be seen in Fig. 1, the C-dots suspensions present a pale orange color under daylight. Interestingly, i-Cdots-LA exhibit intense orange to near-red color, whereas the rest of C-dots are pale orange. The C-dots were analyzed through a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) by dropping 1.0 µL of each colloid onto the copper grid and then allowing them to dry at room temperature. Fig. 2 shows the TEM micrographs for A-Cdots, B-Cdots, C-Cdots, B-Cdots-Cy, and C-Cdots-Cy; it is clearly observed that our method produced C-dots, with estimated sizes of 5.5, 4.3, 6.2, 5.25, and 7 nm, respectively. The insets show the C-dots with different magnifications and the lattice spacing measured (0.270 nm for C-Cdots and 0.185 nm for B-Cdots-Cy) using Image J software.

Furthermore, C-dots were analyzed utilizing Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to investigate the presence of the functional groups at the surface of C-dots. A-Cdots, B-Cdots, and C-Cdots show similar infrared spectroscopic characteristics, and the main peaks are located at 3289 cm−1 (–OH), 2937 cm−1(C–H), 2871 cm−1(C–H), 1652 cm−1(CO), 1548 cm−1 (CN) and 1401 cm−1(C–N). On the other hand, significant variations in the absorbance and position of those peaks were observed in the FTIR spectra of C-dots when they were prepared with the nucleotides or the amino acid. Some additional peaks were observed for C-dots prepared with adenine at 1068 cm−1, assigned to the anti-symmetric stretching mode of (C-O) and assigned to the symmetric stretching mode of 840–836 cm−1; for C-dots prepared with L-arginine at 3344 and 3168 cm−1 assigned to –NH, and 838–840 cm−1, while for C-dots prepared with cytidine at 3340 and 3200 cm−1 assigned to –NH, 1286 cm−1(C–N), 1105 assigned to the anti-symmetric stretching mode of (C-O), and 871 cm−1 assigned to the symmetric stretching mode of (C-O). All these peaks are similar to previous reports [8,25–28]. Fig. 3 shows the characteristic FTIR spectra of C-Cdots, C-Cdots-Ad, C-Cdots-LA, and C-Cdots-Cy. The FTIR results indicate functional groups on the surface of C-dots, such as amino, carbonyl, carboxylate, and hydroxyl groups, which give good dispersibility to C-dots. The reader can review the FTIR analysis for all samples in Fig. S1 in the supplementary material. Besides, XPS analysis of the surface components of the C-dots was carried out to confirm the FTIR results. The XPS spectra are presented in Fig. 3b; in general, the spectra showed four typical peaks: C1s (285 eV), N1s (400 eV), Na Auger (497 eV) and O1s (532 eV). The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s and N1s are shown in Fig. S2 in the supplementary material. For example, the analysis for sample C-Cdots-Cy, which exhibits the best QY, revealed the presence of sp2/sp3 carbons (C–C/CC, 284.8 eV), nitrous carbons (C–N, 286.1 eV) and carbonyl carbons (CO, 287.7 eV). The N1s band was deconvoluted into two peaks at 398.4 and 399.8 eV, corresponding to pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N/-NH, respectively. The O1s band contained peaks at 530.9 and 532.5 eV for (C–O/C–OH) and (CO), respectively. Similar results were obtained for the other three samples: A-Cdots, C-Cdots, and B-Cdots-Cy. Table 1 shows a summary of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen percentage content of samples A-Cdots, C-Cdots, B-Cdots-Cy, and C-Cdots-Cy, showing that samples prepared with Cytidine have higher content of nitrogen compared with those samples prepared without the Cytidine.

The formation of C-dots with BSA is based on the denaturation of protein promoting the loss of solubility and the aggregation of BSA caused by NaOH and ethanol, where the addition of glutaraldehyde generates the formation of imines (CN) groups with the primary amine groups promoted by NaOH [29]. Besides, the amino acid or nucleoside was added during the synthesis to co-dope the C-dots with nitrogen atoms and chromophore fragments by crosslinking the primary amines of Adenosine, Arginine, or Cytidine. Insertion of chromophores fragments as CC, CO, and CN bonds and adding groups such as amines (NH) and alcohol (OH) contribute to the behavior of optical properties of the C-dots. As it was reported, absorption bands below 300 nm correspond to π→ π * transitions corresponding to CC bonds, and bands located between 300 to 400 nm can be assigned to n → π * transition of the CO/CN bonds from the core of C-dots. Meanwhile, the absorption bands above 400 nm originated from n → π * surface state transitions of functional groups like CO, CN, or heavily graphitic N [30–33]. The surface functional groups, such as CO and CN groups, also known as the molecular state, can tune C-dots' fluorescence. These functional surface groups can then introduce new energy levels and produce new electron transitions, which may be related to the fluorescence emission of C-dots [33–35].

Therefore, the optical properties of our C-dots were investigated through UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 4 shows the UV-Vis absorbance spectra of C-dots prepared for the three batches. The C-dots have an intense absorption band from 500 to 550 nm, with maximum absorption center around 536 nm assigned to n→π * electronic transition. A second low-intensity absorption band centered around 654 nm, also assigned to n→π * electronic transition, was observed for sample A-Cdots-j and B-Cdots-j, but not for C-Cdots-j samples; Gaussian deconvolution was carried out on absorbance spectra to determine the wavelength of maximum absorption, and FWHM see Table 2 for more details.

Photoluminescent measurements such as quantum yield, photoluminescent decay lifetimes and spectral emission were carried out on all the C-dot samples. Fig. 5 shows the fluorescent emission maps in panels b to e; the maximum wavelength emission of around 558 nm is localized at the center of each map (see the red zone), excepting samples prepared with L-Arginine. For those samples, the maximum emission was observed around 562 nm for A-Cdots-LA, 569 nm for B-Cdots-LA, and 564 nm for C-Cdots-LA. The reader can review the photoluminescence spectra and excitation-emission maps for all samples in the supplementary material, Figs. S3 and S4. The maximum emission occurred for excitations between 530 to 550 nm for all samples; given that the 532 nm laser line is readily available, we extracted the data for 530 nm excitation from the maps to show the emission spectra of the C-Cdots-j depicted in Fig. 5a.

Fluorescence QY was measured by fixing the excitation also at 530 nm, and the emission at 558 nm. Samples of batch-A show QY values between 22.7% and 27.1%, samples of batch-B between 17.0% and 26.1%, and samples of batch-C between 28.1% and 31.4%. Samples prepared with LA showed a lower QY, while samples prepared with Cy showed the highest QY in each batch. Nevertheless, the C-Cdots-Cy sample had the highest QY (31.4%). Fig. 5f shows a plot of QY behavior for the three batches and Table 2 shows a summary of the luminescent properties of C-dots. In all cases where Cytidine was used, the QY increased compared with the rest of the samples. In fact, the nitrogen content increases for samples prepared with Cytidine compared to those prepared without the nucleoside. These results indicate that nitrogen co-doping the C-dots may improve the photoluminescent properties of carbon dots. Fluorescence decay curves and the optical properties of the Cdots were recorded, as shown in Fig. 6. These curves were fitted by a multiexponential function with two or three fluorescence lifetimes. The multiexponential nature of the fluorescence decay indicated the presence of different emissive sites [36,37].

The fluorescence decay curve was fitted through the equation below:

τ

3

The fluorescence decay curves and parameters of the lifetime measurement for the C-dots are shown in the supplementary information, Fig. S5 and Table S1, respectively. It was observed that B-Cdots-j samples have a longer fluorescence lifetime than C-dots of Batches A and C.

Finally, Fig. 7 shows the colloids of i-Cdots-j fabricated in this work using different nitrogen-containing precursors. All C-dot colloids have an interesting strong yellow fluorescence under green light (532 nm), as was observed from the emission map.

Our C-dots have a moderated QY when compared with nanoparticles of fluorescent polymers [38]. However, it is well known that C-dots are more photostable than commercial dyes [39]. Photostability is the property of a material or a nanomaterial that indicates its stability under exposure to radiation, which can be ultraviolet, visible or infrared. Routinely, fluorescent materials or nanomaterials tend to bleach with time when exposed to intense light. Currently, fluorescent materials, including C-dots, have been proposed in diverse applications such as biomarkers (imaging), photodynamic therapy, light harvesting for solar energy applications, and organic light-emitting diodes [23,40–46]. Therefore, high photostability is required in this kind of application where materials are exposed to light for a long duration. Here, we have also studied the photostability of our carbon dots, as one of their primary applications can be as biological markers. The C-dots prepared with Cytidine show good properties, emissivity and QY. For the photostability study, three samples were prepared with C-dots at a concentration of 0.4 W% in water, and colloidal samples were poured into a quartz cuvette cell (1cmx1cm) and excited under continuous illumination with a 532 nm laser, 33 mW (117 mW/cm2); then, fluorescence spectra were recorded every twenty minutes. Fig. 8 shows normalized fluorescence intensity, and the inset shows optical images of the samples at the beginning and the end of the experiment.

Nonetheless, our C-dots were exposed to intense continuous radiation and decayed at 35% of their original emission; yet, they still exhibited reasonable emission, such as the inset optical image shows.
In this work, we have reported a straightforward preparation method to produce fluorophores with a moderated QY and good photostability at a lower cost than commercial ones.

Herein, we present a facile and general strategy to prepare nitrogen-doped C-dots (N-Cdots) where the carbonization of precursors was not required. Bovine serum albumin was used as the primary carbon source, and L-arginine, Adenine or Cytidine were used as nitrogen sources. The carbon dots of batch A and B show two absorption bands located around 536 and 654 nm, while samples of batch C only show one absorption band around 536 nm. All C-dots exhibited excellent photoluminescent properties with a maximum emission at 558 nm, and had favorable solubility in water due to the presence of CO and O-H functional groups. XPS analysis showed that samples have functional groups like NH2, NH and C-N, which clearly shows that samples prepared with Cytidine show higher nitrogen content than those prepared only with BSA. Besides, samples prepared with Cytidine without ethanol exhibited the highest intense fluorescence of the twelve samples of C-dots that were synthesized; the QY of fluorescence was 31.4%. To the best of our knowledge, this value is among the highest reported for C-dots when using BSA as a precursor of carbon source. On the other hand, photostability under intense green laser irradiation shows good photostability after 180 min and low toxicity, due to the preparation with BSA and water as the primary solvent.

Ojeda-Galvan H J: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Cardoso-Avila P E: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Martínez M M: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Pichardo-Molina Juan Luis: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Aparicio-Ixta L: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Material Characterization Lab (CIO) and M Christian Albort for her technical help, National Laboratory of Nano and Biomaterials (CINVESTAV, Merida, Yuc., Mex.) for their facilities for XPS analysis, Ph.D. G. Ramos for fluorometer access (CIO), Jesus Aparicio Ixta for help in abstract design and Mario Ruiz Verganza for proofreading the manuscript.
